title: Analytical Angle-Finding and Series Expansions for Quantum Signal Processing via Orthogonal Polynomial Theory
abstract: Quantum signal processing is a powerful framework in quantum algorithms, playing a central role in Hamiltonian simulation and related applications. The sequence of polynomials implemented at each step of this protocol provides a polynomial basis for block-encoding any polynomial of a unitary. We characterize the achievable polynomial bases in terms of their orthogonality or biorthogonality with respect to a linear functional admitting an integral representation. Explicit expressions for the quantum signal processing angles are derived for families of polynomial sequences, including Hermite, Jacobi, and Rogers-Szegő polynomials. We show that $2n+2$ rotation angles are required to encode a sequence of polynomials in these classes up to degree $n$. We use this result to show that an $ε$-approximation of a smooth function $f$ can be block-encoded using $O(\log(1/ε))$ gates via its Hermite series expansion. The connections established with the theory of orthogonal and biorthogonal polynomials lead to a new method for solving the quantum signal processing angle-finding problem, yielding explicit expressions for the angles. They also provide a complete characterization of the polynomials achievable by $\mathrm{SU}(1,1)$-QSP in terms of their roots. Biorthogonality properties are shown to hold in the bivariate QSP setting, yielding a set of necessary conditions for achievable polynomials.
summary: This paper provides a mathematical breakthrough for Quantum Signal Processing by replacing numerical angle-finding with analytical solutions derived from orthogonal polynomial theory. It establishes explicit formulas for various polynomial families and demonstrates highly efficient gate complexity for approximating smooth functions.
main_problem: The paper addresses the difficulty of analytically finding the rotation angles required for Quantum Signal Processing (QSP) and seeks to characterize the classes of polynomials achievable in various QSP variants.
main_result: The authors derive explicit analytical expressions for QSP angles using orthogonal polynomial theory and prove that an epsilon-approximation of smooth functions can be achieved with O(log(1/epsilon)) gate complexity using Hermite series expansions.
method: The study utilizes the theory of orthogonal and biorthogonal polynomials (including Hermite, Jacobi, and Rogers-Szego), recurrence relations, and complex analysis (residue calculus) to solve the angle-finding problem.
model_or_system: The framework is based on Quantum Signal Processing (QSP), a quantum algorithmic protocol for implementing polynomial transformations of a unitary operator U, including variants like SU(1,1)-QSP and bivariate QSP.
key_observables: QSP rotation angles (theta and phi), block-encoding constants, and polynomial recurrence coefficients.
important_parameters: Polynomial degree n, error tolerance epsilon, and the growth rate of the normalization constant alpha_n.
main_assumptions: Assumes the existence of quasi-definite linear functionals and that target polynomials have distinct real roots; function approximation assumes specific smoothness/growth constraints.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a generalized QSP circuit iteration; Figure 2 compares LCU architectures; Figure 3 displays the OP-QSP circuit architecture; Figure 5 illustrates bivariate QSP; Figure 6 shows an SU(1,1) rotation implementation circuit.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QSP angle-finding problem, establishes the connection between QSP and orthogonal polynomial theory, provides explicit angle derivations for specific polynomial families, extends the results to SU(1,1) and bivariate QSP, and concludes with complexity analysis for function approximation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
